grammar.usage: Predictions, decisions, intentions, offers, suggestions, requests
grammar.example: Shall I help you with your homework? / I'm going to be a famous astronaut someday!
Are they installing the new computers next week?

grammar.usage: Timetables, fixed arrangements, after if in conditionals, after time conjunctions
grammar.example: The bus to Ben Thanh market leaves at 8 a.m.

grammar.usage: Actions in progress at a specific time in the future, future habits or repeated events
grammar.example: This time next week, I'll be taking my Maths exam.

grammar.usage: Actions completed before a specific time in the future
grammar.example: I will have finished my homework by the time my mum comes home.

grammar.usage: Actions in progress up to a specific time in the future showing duration
grammar.example: At 7 p.m., I will have been doing my Math exercise for three hours!

richGrammar.title: A) WILL / WON'T & BE GOING TO, PRESENT CONTINUOUS & PRESENT SIMPLE

blocks.content: 1. COMPARISON OF WILL / WON'T & BE GOING TO

rows:
More formal situations
EX: The website will come online next week.

Less formal situations
EX: The website is going to come online next week.

Decision made at the moment of speaking
EX: I decided! I won't get new DVD player just yet.

Intention already decided
EX: I decided! I'm not going to get new DVD player just yet.

Offers and suggestions (use shall with I / we)
✓ Shall I help you with your homework?
✗ Will I help you with your homework?
(Do not use will in questions with I / we)

Plans and intentions (already decided)
EX: I'm going to be a famous astronaut someday!

Requests
EX: Will you do washing - up tonight?

First Conditional
EX: If we get a computer, we'll be able to learn this course online.

Predictions based on present evidence (both are okay)
EX: It sounds like the plane's going to take off / will take off in a few minutes.

Predictions based on present evidence (more common for immediate future)
✓ It sounds like the plane's going to take off in a few mins.
✗ It sounds like the plane will take off in a few minutes. (Less natural when immediate)

blocks.content: 2. PRESENT CONTINUOUS & PRESENT SIMPLE AS FUTURE

rows:
Future arrangements confirmed before the moment of speaking
EX: Are they installing the new computers next week?

Timetables, fixed schedules, and arrangements
EX: The bus to Ben Thanh market leaves at 8 a.m.
✗ The bus to Ben Thanh market will leaves at 8 a.m.

Also used after 'if' in zero and first conditionals, and after time conjunctions (when, as soon as, before, etc.)
EX: If technology continues to advance so quickly...
EX: We'll find out as soon as we get to the lab.

bullets: I will come. (no plan, might not arrive)
I'm going to come. (plan exists, but still might not arrive)
I'm coming. (definite, almost certain to arrive)

richGrammar.title: B) FUTURE CONTINUOUS, FUTURE PERFECT SIMPLE & FUTURE PERFECT CONTINUOUS

blocks.content: 1. STRUCTURE OF FUTURE CONTINUOUS, FUTURE PERFECT SIMPLE & FUTURE PERFECT CONTINUOUS

rows: This time next week, I'll be taking my Maths exam.
I will have finished my homework by the time my mum comes home.
At 7 p.m., I will have been doing my Math exercise for three hours!

- Actions in progress at a specific time in the future (two future points: this time of next week & next week)
Note: Next week, I will take my Math exam. (only one future point)

- Future habits or repeated events
EX: In the future, we'll all be flying around using jet - packs.

- Future perfect simple: Actions starting in the present/past and completed before a specific future time.

- Future perfect continuous: Actions starting in the present/past and continuing up to a specific future time showing duration.

bullets: When a time clause begins with: when, as soon as, before, after, until, till, while, once...
Do NOT use WILL in the time clause. Instead, use present simple, present continuous, or present perfect.
EX: We'll find out as soon as we get to the lab. (Not: as soon as we will get...)

rows: on Monday, on my birthday / Easter Day...
next to / beside / by the building
in front of / behind the station

vocabulary.meaning: suitable for official, important, or public occasions; dignified and serious
vocabulary.example: You should wear a formal suit for the job interview.

vocabulary.meaning: having a relaxed, friendly, or unofficial style, manner, or nature
vocabulary.example: We had an informal meeting over coffee to discuss the project.

vocabulary.meaning: the rigid observance of rules of convention or etiquette
vocabulary.example: There was a sense of formality at the royal dinner.

vocabulary.meaning: a small island, especially in a literary or poetic context
vocabulary.example: The castle is located on a beautiful green isle in the middle of the lake.

vocabulary.meaning: a large bus used for long-distance travel, or a person who trains athletes
vocabulary.example: We travelled to London by coach, and our coach gave us advice during the trip.

vocabulary.meaning: having a view of the harbor from above
vocabulary.example: Our hotel room was overlooking the harbour, offering a beautiful view of the ships.

vocabulary.meaning: state or describe exactly the nature, scope, or meaning of something
vocabulary.example: It is difficult to define the exact meaning of this abstract word.

vocabulary.meaning: a brush with a long handle on it for sweeping floors
vocabulary.example: She used a broom to sweep the dust off the kitchen floor.

vocabulary.meaning: a long and distinct period of history with a particular feature or characteristic
vocabulary.example: The invention of the internet marked the beginning of a new technological era.

vocabulary.meaning: the rapid development of industry that occurred in Britain in the late 18th and 19th centuries
vocabulary.example: The industrial revolution changed the way people lived and worked.

vocabulary.meaning: an improvement or development in technology
vocabulary.example: Recent technological advances have made smartphones incredibly powerful.

vocabulary.meaning: insert or fix something in a person's body, especially by surgery
vocabulary.example: The surgeon managed to implant a new pacemaker in the patient's heart.

vocabulary.meaning: a thin plate on which chips and other electronic components are placed
vocabulary.example: The technician replaced the damaged electronic circuit board in the computer.
